methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Silicon Quantum Computing, founded by Michelle Simmons, is a Series A quantum computing company that has raised A$83M to date. The quantum computing sector comprises a limited peer universe of 11 total comparables: 3 public companies (IonQ, Rigetti Computing, and D-Wave Systems) and 8 private firms including IonQ, Quantinuum, Rigetti, D-Wave Quantum, and Infleqtion.

Standard peer-median valuation methodology is not applicable in this instance. The quantum computing sector lacks sufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables with similar valuation tiers to support a robust median analysis. Specifically, zero same-stage peers, one adjacent-stage peer, and zero similar-tier matches were identified—falling short of the minimum three-datapoint threshold required for statistical reliability. Reliance on an all-sector median would introduce material distortion given the heterogeneity of quantum hardware architectures, commercialization timelines, and technology risk profiles across the peer set.

Without triangulated quantitative results from peer multiples, valuation of Silicon Quantum Computing cannot be mechanically anchored to comparable company metrics at this stage. A complete analysis would require either expansion of the dataset through secondary fundraising rounds in quantum hardware, or deployment of complementary methods such as discounted cash flow or venture capital method approaches, neither of which are supported by the data provided. The principal valuation uncertainty remains the sparse and early-stage nature of quantum computing financings.
